coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as multifunctional fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing in ubiquitous water environments and living cells as well as lysine detection via “on-off-on” mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiajia
Luo, Yuanping
Cui, Chen
Han, Qiurui
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Iron and amino acids are essential nutrients for living organisms, and their deficiency or excess can cause a range of diseases. Therefore, there is considerable interest in developing sensing assays capable of detecting these nutrients with sensitivity, selectivity, and multifunctionality even in complex environments. In this report, hydrothermally synthesized blue fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from zinc gluconate were utilized for the detection of Fe3+ and lysine via “on-off” and “on-off-on” mechanisms, respectively. Specifically, the Fe3+ sensing assay achieved a broad linear range of 0–200 μM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.9 μM. It is worth mentioning that the assay was also well adapted to natural aqueous environments (e.g., lake water), and its linear detection range could be extended to 0–1000 μM with a LOD of 3.3 μM. Furthermore, the assay was also effective for intracellular Fe3+ tracking. Most importantly, the assay could also be applied for the quantitative detection of lysine with a linear range of 0–1200 μM and LOD of 8.6 μM. Systematic mechanistic studies revealed that Fe3+ sensing was based on a static quenching process between C-dots and Fe3+, whereas a stronger complexation might have formed between Fe3+ and Lys, leading to the release of C-dots and thus the recovery of fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+ sensing
Lysine detection
Cellular sensing
Quenching mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Applied Basic Research Program of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Z. Peng would like to express gratitude for the funding received from the High-Level Talents Introduction Program in Yunnan Province (No. C619300A081) and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (grant number: 202301AT070147). The authors would also like to thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for providing sample testing services.

Z. Peng would like to express gratitude for the funding received from the High-Level Talents Introduction Program in Yunnan Province (No. C619300A081 ) and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (grant number: 202301AT070147 ). The authors would also like to thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for providing sample testing services.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Standard characterizations of C-dots: (a) TEM image of the C-dots, the lower left inset is a histogram of the grain size distribution of the C-dots, and the upper right insets are plots of the lattice spacing of specific particles under HRTEM; (b) XRD pattern of the C-dots; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots and zinc gluconate, insets are the photos of C-dots aqueous dispersion under ambient light (left) and under a 385 nm UV lamp (right), respectively; (d) Excitation spectrum (red line, λem was set a as constant at 445 nm) and emission spectrum (black line, λex was set as a constant at 360 nm) of C-dots dispersed in water at room temperature; (e) Fluorescent emission intensities of C-dots at different time under continuous xenon light irradiation (blue line in top frame) and fluorescent emission intensities of C-dots dispersed in KCl solutions of different concentrations (brown line in bottom frame); (f) Normalized fluorescent emission intensities of C-dots dispersed in varied pH. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) FTIR spectrum of C-dots, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s and (d) Zn 2p of C-dots, respectively.

Under the optimal conditions, (a) The PL emissions of C-dots dispersed in deionized (DI) water in the presence of Fe3+ of gradient concentrations (0–700 μM); (b) Scatter diagram between F/F0 and concentrations of Fe3+ (0–700 μM); (c) Linear fitting diagram between F/F0 and Fe3+ concentrations (0–200 μM); (d) Normalized fluorescence intensities of blank samples (C-dots) and in the presence of common metal ions (750 μM). From left to right, monovalent metal ions (purple columns): K+, Na+ and Ag+; divalent metal ions (yellow columns): Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Mn2+ and Pb2+; trivalent metal ions (blue columns): Al3+ and Cr3+; Fe3+ coexists with five other cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Al3+, green column). F0 and F denoted the fluorescence emission intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Under the optimal conditions, (a) The PL spectra of C-dots dispersions in lake water with gradient Fe3+ concentrations (0–1000 μM); (b) The linear relationship between C-dots fluorescence intensities and Fe3+ concentrations in lake water, where the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of Fe3+ was denoted as F0 and F, respectively, and [C] was Fe3+ concentration in lake water.

435 s cells incubated with C-dots in the absence (up) and presence (down) of Fe3+ (100 μM), respectively. Images (a) and (c) were under bright field while images (b) and (d) were under green channel excited at 470 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PL decay curves of C-dots only (black) and C-dots + Fe3+ (red and green); (b) Influence of Fe3+ on C-dots UV–vis absorptions, C-dots only (black), Fe3+ (red), C-dots with Fe3+ (blue), and calculated curve (green) by subtracting red curve (Fe3+ only) from C-dots with Fe3+ (blue), respectively; (c) The change of FTIR spectrum of C-dots after the interaction with Fe3+; (d) Stern-Volmer curves at varied temperatures (0, 25 and 60 °C). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram showing Fe3+ quenching C-dots fluorescence and Lys restoring the fluorescence; (b) The recovery of Fe3+-quenched C-dots emissions by the inclusion of Lys with gradient concentrations (0–1200 μM); (c) Linear fitting diagram of normalized C-dots PL intensities (F/F0) and Lys concentrations (0–1200 μM); (d) Interference of other amino acids (1000 μM) on the sensing of Lys; (e) Recoveries of spike Lys solutions in DI water samples based on the DI water derived calibration curves. The fluorescence intensities of blank C-dots dispersion were denoted as F0, and the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the presence of Fe3+ (200 μM) and Lys were denoted as F.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots only (black), 200 μM Fe3++1mM Lys (red), C-dots + 200 μM Fe3++1mM Lys (blue), and the calculated curve (dashed pink) after subtracting the red curve (200 μM Fe3++1mM Lys) from the blue curve (C-dots + 200 μM Fe3++1mM Lys), respectively; (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of 1 mM Lys only (black), 200 μM Fe3+ only (red), 200 μM Fe3++1 mM Lys (blue), and the calculated curve (green) after subtracting the red curve (200 μM Fe3+) from the blue curve (200 μM Fe3++1 mM Lys), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Recoveries of Fe3+ in real water samples based on the DI water derived calibration curve.
Recoveries of Fe3+ in lake water samples based on the lake water derived calibration curves.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as multifunctional fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing in ubiquitous water environments and living cells as well as lysine detection via “on-off-on” mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology
National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electron Microscopy Center, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electron Microscopy Center, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron and amino acids are essential nutrients for living organisms, and their deficiency or excess can cause a range of diseases. Therefore, there is considerable interest in developing sensing assays capable of detecting these nutrients with sensitivity, selectivity, and multifunctionality even in complex environments. In this report, hydrothermally synthesized blue fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from zinc gluconate were utilized for the detection of Fe3+ and lysine via “on-off” and “on-off-on” mechanisms, respectively. Specifically, the Fe3+ sensing assay achieved a broad linear range of 0–200 μM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.9 μM. It is worth mentioning that the assay was also well adapted to natural aqueous environments (e.g., lake water), and its linear detection range could be extended to 0–1000 μM with a LOD of 3.3 μM. Furthermore, the assay was also effective for intracellular Fe3+ tracking. Most importantly, the assay could also be applied for the quantitative detection of lysine with a linear range of 0–1200 μM and LOD of 8.6 μM. Systematic mechanistic studies revealed that Fe3+ sensing was based on a static quenching process between C-dots and Fe3+, whereas a stronger complexation might have formed between Fe3+ and Lys, leading to the release of C-dots and thus the recovery of fluorescence.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron plays a key role for many enzymes thanks to its ability to transform between the two oxidation states (Fe2+ and Fe3+), which are essential for its biological function [1]. For instance, iron is important for oxygen storing and transporting within the body and also helps to regulate tissue homeostasis while enhancing immune function. As such, both iron deficiency and excess can lead to severe cellular damage and a range of diseases [2] including iron-deficiency anemia (IDA) [3], liver damage [4], kidney failure [5], heart disease [6] and cancer [7]. In China, it is required that iron levels in drinking water must not be higher than 3.57 μM [8]. As a result, it is imperative to develop highly sensitive and selective iron-sensing techniques. On the other hand, amino acids are essential components of the human body that play crucial roles in its structure and hormones. A shortage of specific amino acids could lead to weakened immunity, digestive issues, obesity, reproductive problems, decreased mental alertness, delayed childhood development, and other health concerns. Hence, there is also a necessity to develop simple and rapid analysis methods for amino acid determination in routine analyses.

Herein, C-dots based fluorescent probe that realized the sensitive and selective sensing of both Fe3+ and lysine (Lys) via “on–off” and “on–off-on” mechanisms, respectively, was reported in this study. Specifically, for Fe3+ sensing, this assay demonstrated a linear range of 0–200 μM and a low LOD of 1.9 μM in deionized (DI) water. The assay also exhibited high selectivity towards Fe3+ and was not affected by most common interfering ions. In addition to its excellent sensitivity and selectivity, this assay also showed high reliability in tap water and mineral water environments. Furthermore, a calibration curve directly derived from lake water was developed and found to be very effective for Fe3+ sensing in complex lake water environment, achieving a linear range of 0–1000 μM with a LOD of 3.3 μM. The fluorescent detection of Fe3+ by C-dots was attributed to static quenching process induced by the electron interaction between Fe3+ and the hydroxyl group of C-dots. Excitingly, the intracellular sensing of Fe3+ with the same assay was demonstrated to be distinct and effective. Most importantly, the assay was also exploited for the sensing of Lys via an “on–off-on” mechanism, achieving a broad linear range of 0–1200 μM and a low LOD of 8.6 μM. The assay was highly selective towards Lys free from the interference of 17 other amino acids, possibly due to the formation of a strong complex between Fe3+ and Lys, which restored the fluorescence of C-dots.

C-dots were prepared from zinc gluconate as the carbon precursor via a one-step hydrothermal reaction. Briefly, zinc gluconate was dissolved in DI water and left to react in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 24 h. When the reaction was completed and cooled to r.t., the resulted mixture was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 15 min and the dispersion was collected and vacuum dried to result light yellow powders as the end product.

For the Fe3+ sensing assay, a series of FeCl3 solutions of gradient concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700, 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 and 1000 μM) were prepared, then 0.2 mL of the aqueous dispersion of C-dots (3 mg/mL) was added to each of the Fe3+ solutions (2.8 mL). The resulting dispersions were mixed thoroughly and maintained for 3 min at r.t. before the fluorescent emissions (ex. 360 nm) of the samples were tested. The fluorescent emission intensities at 445 nm versus the concentrations of Fe3+ were then plotted to establish the calibration curve for Fe3+ sensing.

For the Lys sensing assay, 0.2 mL of aqueous dispersion of C-dots (3 mg/mL) and 2.7 mL of Fe3+ solution (200 μM) were mixed thoroughly and maintained for 3 min at r.t.. Then, Lys solutions (0.1 mL) of varied concentrations were added to ensure the final concentrations of Lys in the cuvettes were 0, 40, 80, 120, 160, 200, 240, 280, 320, 360, 400, 440, 480, 520, 560, 600, 640, 680, 720, 760, 800, 840, 880, 920, 960, 1000, 1040, 1080, 1120, 1160 and 1200 μM, respectively. The fluorescent emissions (excited at 360 nm) of the dispersions were then collected and emission intensities at 445 nm versus the concentrations of Lys were then plotted to establish the calibration curve for Lys sensing.

The LOD for both assays were determined by the equation below:
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
k
where k represents the slopes of the calibration curves and σ is the standard deviation of 20 blank samples [26].

To evaluate the interference of other ions on the sensing of Fe3+, common metals ions including K+, Na+, Ag+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Al3+ and Cr3+ were mixed with C-dots dispersions and the fluorescent emissions of C-dots with and without these ions were recorded and compared via the similar steps as depicted above.

To evaluate the interference of other amino acids on the selective sensing of Lys, common essential amino acids including tryptophan (Trp), phenylalanine (Phe), threonine (Thr), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), valine (Val) and non-essential amino acids including asparagine (Asn), tyrosine (Tyr), glutamic acid (Glu), histidine (His), glutathione (Gsh), serine (Ser), proline (Pro), aspartic acid (Asp), glutamine (Gln), glycine (Gly) and alanine (Ala) were also tested. The Fe3+-quenched C-dots emissions with and without these amino acids were recorded and compared via the similar steps as depicted above.

To evaluate the probe’s reliability towards Fe3+ detection, Fe3+ spike solutions (50, 100 and 150 μM) in C-dots dispersions of tap, mineral and lake water (0.2 mg/mL) were prepared. Measured Fe3+ concentrations were compared to their known concentrations to evaluate the accuracy of the assay in different water environments. Recoveries of the test were determined by the equation below:
(2)
Recovery
%
=
Fe
3 +
f o u n d
Fe
3 +
a d d e d
× 100 %

C-dots cytotoxicity experiment was performed with MTT assay using human breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-435 s) as the cell model [27]. Specifically, MDA-MB-435 s cells were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium) medium, in which fetal bovine serum (10 %) and penicillin/streptomycin (1 %) were supplemented. During the whole process, temperature was maintained at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2 by a dedicated CO2 incubator.

After MDA-MB-435 s cells were planted in the 96-well plate at 6.3 × 103 cells/well for 24 h when cells reached 70–80 % confluence, 10 μL of C-dots dispersions (0–500 μg/mL) were supplemented to the cells and cultured for 48 h. Then, MTT solution (20 μL, 5 mg/mL) was added to each well and cultured for another 4 h, after which 100 μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the insoluble formazan crystals after the supernatant was removed. The final mixture was then shaken for 15 s at ambient temperature before the optical absorbance at 570 and 630 nm, respectively, was determined by a microplate reader (FC, Thermo, USA).

The MDA-MB-435 s cells were plated in a cover-glass-bottom dish of 24-well plate at 1 × 105 cells/well for 24 h when cells reached 70–80 % confluence, then C-dots dispersions (100 μL, 2 mg/mL) were added into each well and maintained for another 4 h. The cells were then washed with PBS for three times to discard the redundant C-dots and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution (15 min) before washed three more times with PBS. To demonstrate the cellular sensing of Fe3+, 1 mL of Fe3+ solution (100 μM) was added and maintained for 30 min, then cells were washed with PBS for three times to discard the redundant C-dots. The cells were further fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution (15 min), followed by three washes with PBS. The staining effects of C-dots on the cells were then evaluated by a Nikon inverted fluorescence microscope.

Quenching of a fluorophore generally occurred via two mechanisms, namely dynamic collision and static complexation. Dynamic quenching could be evaluated with the Stern-Volmer equation below [28]:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
which relates the fluorescence intensity before (F0) and after (F) the addition of the quencher. The equation incorporates the concentration of the quencher ([Q]) and the rate constant for dynamic quenching (Kq); τ0 is the intrinsic fluorophore lifetime in the absence of the quencher.

The C-dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal treatment of zinc gluconate and obtained as pale yellow powders. To characterize the sample, we first surveyed the morphological feature with the aid of TEM microscopy. Clearly, the C-dots appeared as well-dispersed spherical particles (Fig. 1
a) thanks to their negatively charged surfaces (zeta potential –13.0 mV, Fig. S1 ). As presented in the histogram of the size distribution, the diameters of C-dots were mostly located between 1.5 and 4.0 nm, with an average diameter calculated to be 2.8 nm (Fig. 1 a, left inset). The high- resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1 a, right insets) obtained from these C-dots displayed two types of lattices with spacing of 0.27 and 0.37 nm, which could be attributed to the (020) and (002) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon, respctively [29,30]. In addition, the C-dots exhibited two sets of peaks in their XRD pattern (Fig. 1 b). Specifically, the broad peak around 22.2° was commonly seen for C-dots and generally associated with the typical carbonized structure of C-dots [31]. On the other hand, the narrow peaks at 9.4, 17.2, 20.9 and 29.8° were assigned to the characteristic peaks of the carbon precursor zinc gluconate [32], suggesting that C-dots inherited some intrinsic structural and chemical features from the precursor. Taking into account the TEM and XRD analyses altogether, it was reasonable to assume that the carbon precursor zinc gluconate was successfully carbonized and C-dots were indeed formed.

We then carefully evaluated the optical properties of C-dots. The C-dots dispersion was grayish under ambient light (Fig. 1 c, left inset), and became bright blue by UV excitation at 385 nm (Fig. 1 c, right inset). From the UV–vis absorption spectrum, it was clear that the C-dots had strong absorptions at 260 and 335 nm (Fig. 1 c, red spectrum), corresponding to the CC bond π-π* transition and n-π* transition of the CO bond [33], respectively. Both of the two absorption peaks might be ascribed to the result of carbonization, as they were not present in the carbon precursor (Fig. 1 c, blue spectrum). Subsequently, it can be seen in the excitation and emission spectra of C-dots that the C-dots dispersion could be effectively excited by light in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm (Fig. 1 d, red curve); under the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the optimal fluorescence emission was observed at 445 nm (Fig. 1 d, black curve). Furthermore, the C-dots displayed typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emissions: as the excitation wavelengths were incrementally increased from 300 to 400 nm, the emitted fluorescence shifted from 440 to 500 nm ( Fig. S2 ). The absolute PLQY of C-dots under 360 nm excitation was determined to be 8.3 % ( Fig. S3 ).

To our delight, it was demonstrated that the C-dots could withstand high ion strength without losing their fluorescent emissions. More specifically, no noticeable fluorescent emission intensity variations were observed for C-dots when they were dispersed in aqueous solutions of increased KCl concentrations from 0 to 1000 mM (Fig. 1 e, brown line in bottom frame). Furthermore, the C-dots also demonstrated high level of anti-photobleaching capability: there was no sign of PL decays for the C-dots under continuous irradiations for 100 min by a xenon light (Fig. 1 e, blue line in top frame). According to earlier reports [34], C-dots PL were easily influenced by pH changes in environment. Thus, we also explored the influence of pH on the PL intensity ratios of the C-dots. As demonstrated, the emissions of C-dots were generally stable unless the dispersion solutions were extremely acidic (i.e., pH < 2), and maximum performances were assured if the pH was between 6 and 9 (Fig. 1 f, green columns). These properties are considered to be superior for the development of fluorescent sensing assays from C-dots.

Subsequently, we also performed FTIR and XPS studies to evaluate the surface chemical compositions and functional groups of C-dots. In the FTIR spectrum, the peaks at 3443, 1599 and 1110 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2
a). The peaks at 1386 and 615 cm−1 were ascribed to the CH2/Zn = O and Zn-O, indicating the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups. The XPS survey spectrum displayed three peaks at 285, 532 and 1022 eV, which were ascribed to C 1 s, O 1 s and Zn 2p, respectively ( Fig. S4 ). The obtained peaks were deconvoluted further to explore the surface functionalities, and the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum revealed the presence of four types of carbon including OCO at 288.8 eV, CO/CN at 286.9 eV, CO at 286.1 eV and graphitic carbon (CC/CC) at 284.8 eV (Fig. 2 b). Two deconvoluted peaks at 533.4 and 532.0 eV were observed for O 1 s, which were attributed to CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2 c). The high-resolution XPS spectral of C 1 s and O 1 s indicated that there were rich oxygen-containing functionalities present on C-dots surface, which was in high accordance with the FTIR analysis. The two peaks at 1022.0 and 1045.1 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Zn 2p both indicated the existence of Zn2+ (Fig. 2 d) [35].

With the successful optimization of the parameters, we then began to investigate the correlation between C-dots PL intensities and concentrations of Fe3+. As shown, the two demonstrated an obvious negative correlation in which the PL intensities of C-dots rapidly declined as Fe3+ concentrations gradually increased from 0 to 700 μM (Fig. 3
a). A graph with concentrations of Fe3+ on the X-axis and normalized PL intensities of C-dots on the Y-axis was then plotted, and these two parameters were found to be linearly correlated in the range of 0–200 μM (Fig. 3 b). A standard calibration curve was established on this basis and the fitted equation was F/F0 = -0.0023[C] + 0.99, where [C] represented Fe3+ concentration, F0 and F stand for PL intensities of blank C-dots and C-dots with Fe3+, respectively (Fig. 3 c). As demonstrated, the assay had an excellent linearity from 0 to 200 μM as indicated by the near perfect R2 value (0.99). Applying equation
(1) discussed above [26,36], LOD of the sensing system was obtained as 1.90 μM, which is much lower than the allowed Fe3+ presence in drinking water (3.57 μM) in China [8].

Since the coexistence of multiple metal ions in aqueous environment is very common, the selective detection of targeted metal ion without the interference from other ions is especially crucial. We were pleased to find that the assay could efficiently avoid the influence of most common metal ions. Specifically, K+, Na+ and Ag+ (monovalent ions) exhibited little effect on C-dots PL intensity (Fig. 3 d, purple columns). Similarly, eleven common divalent metal ions including Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Mn2+ and Pb2+ did not interfere Fe3+ sensing (Fig. 3 d, yellow columns). Moreover, it is exciting that Fe2+ did not significantly interfere with Fe3+ sensing, which should find important applications where the differentiation of Fe3+ and Fe2+ was important [37]. Furthermore, common trivalent metal ions (i.e., Al3+, Cr3+) were also tested and exhibited no obvious interference with Fe3+ sensing (Fig. 3 d, blue columns). Encouraged by these finding, we proceeded for Fe3+ sensing in the coexistence of five other metal cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Al3+). To our delight, Fe3+ could be facilely detected without obvious interference (Fig. 3 d, green column), demonstrating the high selectivity of the assay towards Fe3+.

Apart from excellent selectivity, reliable detection of the targeted analyte is also of great importance for a sensing assay. As such, Fe3+ spike solutions in various water samples were prepared and experimental concentrations of these solutions determined by the standard calibration curve were carefully analyzed. We were pleased to report that the assay was highly reliable towards the detection of Fe3+ in a variety of water environments (i.e., DI, tap and mineral water). Recoveries for the tested spike solutions were between 98.36 % and 105.00 % (Table 1 , entries 1–6), all within the allowable range. Worth mentioning, the determined concentrations of tap and mineral water spike solutions were extremely close to their actual concentrations (Table 1, entries 3–6, less than 3 % variations), highlighting the excellent practicality of the assay towards the sensing and detection of Fe3+ in non-controlled water environments.

To our surprise, the standard calibration curve developed from DI water was not suitable for Fe3+ detection in lake water. As demonstrated, the calculated concentrations were significantly lower than the actual concentrations (Table 1 , entries 7–8). The distorted recoveries of the lake water samples might be attributed to the fact that certain anions (i.e., CO3 2– and HPO4 2-) in the lake water could promote Fe3+ hydrolysis, accelerate Fe3+ precipitation and thus reduce the effective Fe3+ concentration. This phenomenon is well known and often utilized for the removal of Fe3+ from water bodies [38]. Indeed, when we compared the hydrolysis rates of Fe3+ in DI water and lake water, it was clear that Fe3+ hydrolyzed much faster in lake water as orange colloids (hydrolysis product) rapidly developed in the lake water upon addition of Fe3+ while no obvious colloids were observed in DI water under identical conditions ( Fig. S7 ).

Although application of the DI water-derived standard calibration curve failed for lake water samples, to our delight, an attempt to develop a new calibration curve directly from lake water was extremely successful. Specifically, the PL intensities of C-dots dispersions in lake water at 440 nm rapidly declined as the concentrations of Fe3+ increased (Fig. 4
a). A graph with concentrations of Fe3+ on the X-axis and normalized PL intensities of C-dots on the Y-axis was plotted, in which these two parameters were linearly correlated in the range of 0–200 and 200–1000 μM, respectively (Fig. 4 b). Two standard calibration curves were then established with fitted regression equations of F/F0 = -0.0015[C] + 0.96 and F/F0 = -0.00058[C] + 0.72 in the range of 0–200 μM and 200–1000 μM, respectively. The LOD of the assay was determined to be 3.3 μM with the 3σ method as described above. Excitingly, the lake water-derived calibration curves were extremely reliable towards the sensing of Fe3+ in lake water samples. As shown (Table 2 ), the determined recoveries were between 96.49 and 105.84 %, well within the tolerance range, indicating that the C-dots derived assay could be a great option for sensing and detecting of Fe3+ in lake water environment, which highlights the appealing potential of C-dots as Fe3+ sensing platforms in other complex water environments.

Encouraged by the excellent performance of aqueous Fe3+ sensing, we further applied the assay for the intracellular detection of Fe3+. To begin with, we implemented MTT assay to evaluate C-dots cytotoxicity towards 435 s and BMSCs cells. As demonstrated, the C-dots could be regarded as non-cytotoxic as no obvious cell death was observed for both 435 s and BMSCs cells when C-dots concentrations were less than 62.5 μg/mL, and the cell survival rates could be maintained above 80 % even when C-dots concentration reached 500 μg/mL ( Fig. S8 ). Next, 435 s cells were selected as the model cell for intracellular Fe3+ detection. As a control, C-dots were first cultured with 435 s cells and clear green emissions were observed in the cell membranes and cytoplasm when excited at 470 nm (Fig. 5
b). Upon the introduction of Fe3+ into the culture medium, the bright green fluorescence of 435 s cells was quickly turned off (Fig. 5 d), indicating that the C-dots with favorable cell permeability and excellent PL were capable of tracking Fe3+ dynamics in living cells.

Quenching of C-dots PL could occur via static quenching by forming complex at the ground state or dynamic quenching via collisional deactivation. In a static quenching process, C-dots may undergo interactions with quenchers, leading to the formation of ground-state, non-fluorescent complexes, which remain nonfluorescent and return to ground state after activation without emitting any photons [39]. In a typical static quenching, we could generally expect: 1) the fluorescent lifetime of C-dots remains essentially constant in the presence of a quencher; 2) the appearance of new UV–vis absorption peaks that are different from those of pristine C-dots due to the complexation of C-dots with the quencher; 3) compared to dynamic quenching, temperature normally has less effect on static quenching as the stability of non-fluorescent complex is less influenced by temperature; 4) according to the Stern-Vølmer equation [40], the presumed quenching rate constant (Kq) of a dynamic quenching process is assumed to be within the theoretical range of 1 ∼ 2 × 1010 L·mol-1s−1. Thus, based on these expected phenomena, several experiments were carried out to investigate the quenching mechanism.

To begin with, the fluorescent decay curves of C-dots with and without Fe3+ were determined, which were always double-exponential with no observable drift (Fig. 6
a), and the calculated fluorescent lifetimes remained largely unchanged as 3.43, 3.34 and 3.22 ns for C-dots, C-dots with 200 μM of Fe3+ and C-dots with 400 μM of Fe3+, respectively ( Table S2 ). As the fluorescent lifetimes of C-dots stayed essentially unchanged (within 3 % difference) with or without Fe3+, the quenching was most likely to be static complexation rather than collisional inactivation.

Next, C-dots UV–vis absorption spectrum in the presence of Fe3+ were further analyzed (Fig. 6 b), in which a new peak at 344 nm appeared probably due to the complexation of C-dots with Fe3+ (green curve vs. black curve). Furthermore, the FTIR peaks for -C-O at 1110 cm−1 and Zn-O at 615 cm−1 of C-dots were significantly weakened upon the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 6 c). This might be attributed to the formation of C-dots/Fe3+ complex potentially via the interaction of Fe3+ with the oxygen-containing functionalities of C-dots surface. Collectively consider the above-discussed experiment results, we cautiously propose that C-dots PL quenching by Fe3+ most likely went through the static quenching process, in which the abundant oxygen-containing groups of C-dots acted as the electron donor while Fe3+ with empty d orbitals as the electron acceptor to form non-fluorescent, ground-state complexes [41].

To further validate the above hypothesis, the temperature effect was carefully evaluated. It is well known that the Stern-Vølmer curve of the dynamic quenching is sensitive to temperature while the static quenching is not, since temperature could have a significant impact on the dynamic collision rate of quencher and fluorophore, but has little effect on the formation and stability of static complexes [40]. In the variable temperature experiments, it was evidently shown that the Stern-Vølmer quenching curves at 0, 25 and 60 °C were very similar (Fig. 6 d), further supporting the quenching process was indeed via the static complexation, rather than dynamic collisions. Lastly, the presumed dynamic quenching constant Kq calculated from Fig. 3c was 2.043 × 1011 L·mol-1s−1 by applying the Stern-Volmer relationship (equations
(3) , which was much larger than the theoretical dynamic quenching value (1 ∼ 2 × 1010 L·mol-1s−1) [40], implying the possibility of charge transfer between C-dots and Fe3+ via dynamic quenching was low.

In addition to the excellent Fe3+ sensing performance for both aqueous and intracellular samples, we were excited to report that C-dots also functioned as an exceptional sensor for amino acid Lys based on an “on–off-on” mechanism (Fig. 7
a). Specifically, the PL of C-dots that had already been quenched by Fe3+ rapidly recovered with the increase of Lys concentrations (Fig. 7 b). And clearly the recovery of C-dots PL was linearly related to the concentrations of Lys from 0 to 1200 μM (Fig. 7 c). The calibration curve was established with a standard equation of F/F0 = 0.34 + 0.00051[C] (R2 = 0.98), and a low LOD of 8.6 μM was obtained by following the 3σ method as discussed above.

In biological environments, many amino acids often coexist, thus it is very crucial to prevent interference from other amino acids when performing Lys sensing. To our delight, the assay was highly sensitive towards Lys, free from the interference of essential amino acids including Trp, Phe, Thr, Ile, Leu and Val (Fig. 7 d, green columns). Additionally, other common nonessential amino acids such as Asn, Tyr, Glu, His, Gsh, Ser, Pro, Asp, Gln, Gly and Ala also exhibited little interference to the sensing of Lys (Fig. 7 d, purple columns). To validate the accuracy of the sensing assay, the calculated Lys concentrations were closely compared with the true concentrations. We were pleased to report that the assay was highly reliable. In particular, the recoveries of all the spike samples were between 95.67 and 108.81 % (Fig. 7 e), highlighting the good reliability of this assay for Lys sensing.

The recovery of C-dots PL was attributed to the release of C-dots from C-dots-Fe3+ complex due to the stronger interaction of Fe3+ and Lys [42,43]. To probe this hypothesis, we carefully analyzed the UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots, Fe3+, Lys as well as their mixture (Fig. 8 ). As shown, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of free C-dots (Fig. 8 a, black curve) overlapped very well with that of C-dots in the presence of Fe3+ and Lys (Fig. 8 a, pink dashed curve), indicating that there was no strong interaction of C-dots with Fe3+ and Lys. On the other hand, we have shown above that C-dots most likely have complexed with Fe3+ as the UV–vis absorption spectrum of free C-dots (Fig. 6 b, black curve) and that of C-dots in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 6 b, green curve) were vastly different. Clearly, the complexation of C-dots with Fe3+ quenched their PL, and the stronger interaction of Lys with Fe3+ destroyed or at least weakened this complexation, and thus restored the PL of C-dots. Indeed, a close look at the UV–vis absorption spectra of free Lys (Fig. 8 b, black curve) and Lys in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 8 b, green curve) did show that a strong interaction most likely existed between the two. These studies supported the conclusion that Fe3+ initially formed a non-fluorescent complex with C-dots, which was de-complexed upon the introduction of Lys [44].

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a simple and efficient hydrothermal method for the preparation of C-dots using zinc gluconate as the carbon source. The C-dots exhibited excitation-wavelength-dependent emissions, with the optimal emission at 445 nm effectively quenched by Fe3+. Exploiting this phenomenon, an effortless sensing platform predicated on C-dots was subsequently devised for the precise Fe3+ sensing, which achieved an extensive linear range of 0–200 μM and a low LOD of 1.9 μM. The assay additionally demonstrated exceptional selectivity for Fe3+ and was devoid of interference of most common metal ions. What’s more, the fluorescent probe demonstrated excellent capability in detecting Fe3+ in ubiquitous water environments (i.e., tap water and mineral water) with precision and could still attain high detection accuracy for dissolved Fe3+ in lake environments where some of Fe3+ is deposited. In addition, we also utilized the strong affinity interaction between Fe3+ and Lys to achieve specific detection of Lys with a broad linear range of 0 to 1200 μM and a LOD of 8.6 μM. From the above, in conjunction with its commendable performance in detecting Fe3+ in biological cells, the assay demonstrated immense promise for the detection of Fe3+ and Lys in complex environments. Ultimately, predicated on fluorescence lifetime assays, UV–vis spectroscopy, temperature-induced effects on quenching and theoretical computations, we ascertained that the C-dots predominantly quenched fluorescence by engendering a non-luminescent complex with Fe3+ via a static process, which could be destroyed by the competitive interaction of Lys with Fe3+.

Jiajia Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yuanping Luo: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Chen Cui: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Qiurui Han: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Zhili Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

The authors would also like to thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for providing sample testing services.
